seoTitle: Linco Motor Control MCU Products | BLDC Controllers | LiTong Distributor
seoDescription: Explore Linco motor control MCUs including LKS03, LKS05, LKS07 series for BLDC motor applications. Authorized distributor with technical support.

faqs.question: What are the main product categories offered by Linco?
faqs.answer: Linco offers four main product categories for motor control applications: (1) Motor Control MCUs - the LKS series including LKS03 (compact), LKS05 (cost-effective), LKS06 (dedicated motor control), LKS07 (high-performance with larger memory), and LKS45 (dual motor + PFC) series; (2) Automotive Grade MCUs - the LKS32AT series with AEC-Q100 certification for automotive applications; (3) Gate Drivers - high and low voltage pre-drivers for MOSFET/IGBT power stages; (4) Power Management ICs - integrated LDOs, DC-DC converters, and voltage regulators. All products are designed specifically for BLDC motor control with integrated analog front-end and DSP capabilities.
faqs.decisionGuide: Review each product category to find the Linco solution matching your motor type, performance requirements, and application environment.

faqs.question: How do I select the right Linco MCU for my BLDC motor application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right Linco MCU requires considering: (1) Motor type - PMSM for FOC control or trapezoidal BLDC for six-step commutation; (2) Control complexity - sensorless FOC requires higher performance (LKS07/LKS08) than simple BLDC (LKS03/LKS05); (3) CPU performance - 48MHz for basic applications, 96MHz for complex algorithms, 192MHz for dual motor; (4) Memory needs - Flash size from 32KB to 128KB depending on algorithm complexity; (5) Analog requirements - 12-bit vs 14-bit ADC, number of PGA channels for current sensing; (6) Integration - built-in gate drivers reduce BOM (LKS08); (7) Operating environment - consumer (-40°C to +105°C) vs automotive (-40°C to +150°C); (8) Cost target - LKS03 for high volume cost-sensitive applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use our MCU selection guide or contact FAE with your motor specifications for personalized recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the difference between LKS03, LKS05, and LKS07 series?
faqs.answer: Linco LKS series differentiation: (1) LKS03 - Compact series with 48MHz CPU, 1Msps 12-bit ADC, basic motor control features, smallest QFN20 3x3mm package, best for simple BLDC applications and space-constrained designs; (2) LKS05 - Cost-effective series with 96MHz CPU, 3Msps dual-sampling ADC, enhanced DSP capabilities, balanced performance and price for mainstream motor control; (3) LKS07 - High-performance series with 96MHz CPU + dedicated DSP, 3Msps ADC, larger Flash/RAM, supports complex FOC algorithms, dual motor capability, rich analog peripherals for advanced applications. Choose LKS03 for cost/size, LKS05 for balanced value, LKS07 for high performance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose LKS03 for simple BLDC and cost sensitivity. Choose LKS05 for balanced performance. Choose LKS07 for complex FOC and high performance.

faqs.question: Which Linco MCUs are suitable for automotive applications?
faqs.answer: For automotive applications, Linco offers the LKS32AT series with AEC-Q100 Grade 1 certification: (1) LKS32AT037 - Compact automotive MCU with 48MHz ARM Cortex-M0, integrated 4-phase half-bridge driver, LIN PHY, 5V LDO, ideal for HVAC actuators and small motors; (2) LKS32AT039 - Enhanced automotive MCU with more I/O and higher current drivers for medium power motors; (3) LKS32AT085 - High-performance automotive MCU with 96MHz CPU, 3Msps ADC, rich analog peripherals for complex motor control; (4) LKS32AT086 - Premium automotive MCU with integrated 6N gate driver, highest integration for reduced BOM. All automotive MCUs operate -40°C to +150°C with PPAP documentation available.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select based on motor power and integration requirements. Contact FAE for automotive-specific recommendations and qualification support.

faqs.question: What motor control algorithms are supported by Linco MCUs?
faqs.answer: Linco MCUs support comprehensive motor control algorithms: (1) BLDC Trapezoidal - six-step commutation with Hall sensor or sensorless BEMF detection, supported by all LKS series; (2) FOC (Field Oriented Control) - sensorless and sensored FOC for PMSM motors, requires LKS05/LKS07/LKS08 for sufficient processing power; (3) Stepper Motor Control - microstepping control for stepper motors; (4) Dual Motor Control - simultaneous control of two motors with PFC, supported by LKS45 series; (5) Advanced Features - MTPA (Maximum Torque Per Ampere), flux weakening, vibration suppression, active damping. Linco provides complete algorithm libraries and reference implementations for all supported control methods.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose LKS03 for simple BLDC. Choose LKS05/LKS07 for FOC applications. Contact FAE for algorithm selection and implementation support.

categories.name: Motor Control MCUs
categories.description: Linco motor control MCUs provide integrated solutions for BLDC motor applications. The LKS series combines high-performance CPU cores, dedicated DSP for motor control, rich analog peripherals, and optional gate drivers in a single chip.
categories.longDescription: Linco motor control MCUs are designed specifically for BLDC motor drive applications. As an authorized Linco distributor, LiTong provides comprehensive MCU selection guidance, motor control algorithm support, and application engineering services. The product range includes the LKS03 compact series, LKS05 cost-effective series, LKS07 high-performance series, and LKS45 dual motor series. These MCUs feature integrated instrument-grade PGAs, high-speed ADCs, dedicated motor control DSP, and support various control algorithms including FOC and trapezoidal BLDC.

series.name: LKS03 Series
series.description: Compact motor control MCUs for simple BLDC applications

series.name: LKS05 Series
series.description: Cost-effective MCUs with enhanced performance for mainstream applications

series.name: LKS07 Series
series.description: High-performance MCUs with dedicated DSP for complex FOC algorithms

series.name: LKS45 Series
series.description: Dual motor control MCUs with PFC support for advanced applications

selectionGuide.title: How to Select Linco Motor Control MCUs
selectionGuide.description: Consider motor type, control algorithm, performance requirements, and integration needs when selecting Linco MCUs.

faqs.question: What is the CPU performance of Linco motor control MCUs?
faqs.answer: Linco motor control MCU CPU performance varies by series: (1) LKS03 - 48MHz 32-bit CPU with hardware divide (640ns) and square root (320ns), suitable for simple BLDC control; (2) LKS05/LKS06/LKS07/LKS08 - 96MHz 32-bit CPU with dedicated DSP co-processor, hardware trigonometric functions in 100ns, supports complex FOC algorithms; (3) LKS45 - 192MHz ARM Cortex-M4F with hardware FPU and DSP instructions, supports dual motor + PFC. All CPUs feature single-cycle multiply and hardware divide acceleration for fast motor control loop execution.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select 48MHz LKS03 for simple applications, 96MHz LKS05/LKS07 for FOC, 192MHz LKS45 for dual motor.

faqs.question: What analog peripherals are integrated in Linco MCUs?
faqs.answer: Linco MCUs integrate rich analog peripherals for motor control: (1) PGA (Programmable Gain Amplifier) - instrument-grade fully differential PGA with programmable gain, handles positive/negative signals without bias voltage; (2) ADC - 12-bit to 14-bit resolution, 1Msps to 3Msps sampling rate, dual simultaneous sampling for current measurement; (3) Voltage Reference - 0.5% precision voltage reference across full temperature range; (4) Comparators - multiple high-speed comparators for overcurrent protection and zero-cross detection; (5) Op-amps - integrated operational amplifiers for signal conditioning. These integrated analog peripherals eliminate external components and reduce system cost.
faqs.decisionGuide: All LKS series include integrated analog front-end. LKS07/LKS08 offer the richest analog peripheral set for complex applications.

faqs.question: What packages are available for Linco motor control MCUs?
faqs.answer: Linco motor control MCUs are available in various packages: (1) QFN20 3x3mm - ultra-compact package for LKS03 series, ideal for space-constrained designs like small fans and toys; (2) QFN24/QFN32 - compact packages for LKS05/LKS07 with good thermal performance; (3) QFN48/QFN52 - larger packages for LKS08/LKS45 with more I/O pins and integrated gate drivers; (4) LQFP48/LQFP64 - traditional leaded packages for easy prototyping and manufacturing. All packages support -40°C to +105°C (consumer) or -40°C to +150°C (automotive) operation. QFN packages offer best thermal and electrical performance for motor control applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use QFN20 for compact designs. Use QFN48/QFN52 for high integration with gate drivers. Use LQFP for prototyping.

faqs.question: What development tools are supported by Linco MCUs?
faqs.answer: Linco MCUs support industry-standard development tools: (1) IDEs - Keil MDK-ARM and IAR Embedded Workbench with device packs for all LKS series; (2) Debuggers - J-Link, ULINK, and ST-Link compatible; (3) Development Boards - evaluation kits available for each series with motor power stage and debugging interface; (4) Software Libraries - complete motor control firmware libraries including FOC, BLDC six-step, and stepper control; (5) Code Examples - reference implementations for air conditioner, washing machine, power tool, and fan applications; (6) Documentation - comprehensive datasheets, user manuals, and application notes. Linco also provides technical training and hands-on workshops.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Keil or IAR for development. Order Linco evaluation kits through LiTong for quick prototyping.

faqs.question: How do Linco MCUs handle motor protection and safety?
faqs.answer: Linco MCUs integrate comprehensive motor protection features: (1) Overcurrent Protection - fast analog comparators with hardware shutdown in microseconds; (2) Overvoltage/Undervoltage Protection - programmable voltage monitoring for system safety; (3) Overtemperature Protection - internal temperature sensor with configurable thresholds; (4) Stall Detection - software algorithms detect motor stall and trigger protection; (5) Open Phase Detection - identifies disconnected motor phases; (6) Hardware Fault Input - dedicated pins for external fault signals; (7) Watchdog Timer - system reset on software malfunction. These protection features ensure safe and reliable motor operation in all conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: All LKS series include comprehensive protection. Configure protection thresholds based on your motor and application requirements.

products.name: Compact Motor Control MCU
products.shortDescription: 48MHz motor control MCU with 32KB Flash, integrated PGA and 1Msps ADC in compact QFN20 package.
descriptionParagraphs: The LKS32MC033H6P8 is a compact motor control MCU designed for cost-sensitive BLDC motor applications.
Featuring a 48MHz 32-bit CPU with hardware acceleration for divide and square root operations, this MCU provides sufficient performance for trapezoidal BLDC control.
The integrated instrument-grade PGA and 1Msps 12-bit ADC enable precise current sensing for sensorless motor control.

faeReview.author: Michael Chen
faeReview.title: FAE Engineer - Motor Control
faeReview.content: The LKS32MC033H6P8 is my go-to recommendation for cost-sensitive BLDC applications under 100W. The QFN20 3x3mm package is incredibly compact - perfect for small fans and portable devices where PCB space is limited. The integrated PGA eliminates external op-amps, saving both cost and space. I've used this MCU in numerous small fan projects and it consistently delivers reliable sensorless BLDC control. The 48MHz CPU is sufficient for six-step commutation, though you'll want to step up to LKS05/LKS07 for FOC applications. One tip: the internal RC oscillator is accurate to ±1% across temperature, so you can often eliminate the external crystal for even more cost savings. At this price point with these features, it's hard to beat for simple motor control.
faeReview.highlight: Ultra-compact QFN20 with integrated PGA for cost-sensitive BLDC applications

alternativeParts.reason: Linco offers integrated PGA and motor control features at lower cost
Linco provides higher performance and smaller package

faqs.question: What motor control algorithms can LKS32MC033H6P8 implement?
faqs.answer: The LKS32MC033H6P8 supports trapezoidal BLDC control algorithms: (1) Six-step commutation with Hall sensors - simplest method using Hall effect sensors for rotor position; (2) Sensorless BEMF detection - detects zero-crossing of back-EMF for position estimation, most common for fans and pumps; (3) Simple scalar V/Hz control - for induction motors. The 48MHz CPU and hardware divide/sqrt acceleration provide sufficient performance for these algorithms. However, Field Oriented Control (FOC) requires more processing power - use LKS05/LKS07 series for FOC applications. Linco provides complete reference code for all supported algorithms.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use LKS32MC033 for simple BLDC. Upgrade to LKS05/LKS07 for FOC or complex control.

faqs.question: How accurate is the internal oscillator for motor control?
faqs.answer: The LKS32MC033H6P8 internal RC oscillator provides excellent accuracy for motor control: (1) Factory calibrated to ±1% at 25°C; (2) Temperature drift ±1% across -40°C to +105°C range; (3) Total accuracy ±2% worst-case across temperature and voltage; (4) Sufficient for BLDC commutation timing which tolerates 5-10% timing variation. For most fan and pump applications, the internal oscillator eliminates the need for an external crystal, reducing BOM cost. However, for applications requiring precise speed control or serial communication at high baud rates, consider using an external crystal or upgrading to LKS05/LKS07 with better oscillator specs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Internal oscillator sufficient for most BLDC. Use external crystal for precise speed control.

faqs.question: What is the current sensing capability with integrated PGA?
faqs.answer: The integrated PGA in LKS32MC033H6P8 provides: (1) Fully differential input - handles bipolar current signals without external level shifting; (2) Programmable gain - typically 1x to 32x configurable for different current ranges; (3) High input impedance - minimizes loading on current sense resistors; (4) Low offset voltage - ensures accurate current measurement at low currents; (5) Bandwidth - sufficient for motor current sampling at PWM switching frequencies up to 20kHz. The PGA output connects directly to the internal ADC. For single-shunt or dual-shunt current sensing topologies, the integrated PGA eliminates 2-3 external op-amps, saving significant BOM cost and PCB space.
faqs.decisionGuide: Integrated PGA supports single/dual shunt sensing. Configure gain based on current range.

faqs.question: How do I debug and program the LKS32MC033H6P8?
faqs.answer: Debugging and programming the LKS32MC033H6P8: (1) Programming interface - SWD (Serial Wire Debug) interface with 2 pins (SWDIO, SWCLK); (2) Debuggers supported - J-Link, ULINK, ST-Link, and compatible debuggers; (3) IDE support - Keil MDK with Linco device pack, IAR Embedded Workbench; (4) Flash programming - via debugger or serial bootloader; (5) Real-time debugging - breakpoints, single-step, variable watch, memory view; (6) Trace capabilities - limited trace buffer for debugging. The QFN20 package requires careful PCB layout for debug signal access. Linco evaluation boards include standard 10-pin SWD connectors for easy debugging.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use J-Link or compatible debugger with Keil MDK. Order evaluation board for easy debugging.

faqs.question: What is the ESD protection level and why does it matter?
faqs.answer: The LKS32MC033H6P8 features robust ESD protection: (1) HBM (Human Body Model) - 6kV protection on all pins, exceeds industry standard 2kV; (2) Air discharge - 15kV protection per IEC 61000-4-2; (3) Contact discharge - 8kV protection. This high ESD immunity is critical for motor control applications because: (1) Motors generate electrical noise and transients; (2) User touch interfaces require protection; (3) Industrial environments have high EMI; (4) Reduces field failures and warranty returns. The strong ESD protection eliminates external protection components in many applications, reducing BOM cost. This is especially important for consumer products where users may touch control panels.
faqs.decisionGuide: High ESD protection suitable for consumer and industrial motor control without external protection.

products.description: 48MHz motor control MCU with 32KB Flash, integrated PGA and 1Msps ADC in compact QFN20 package.

products.name: High-Performance Motor Control MCU
products.shortDescription: 96MHz motor control MCU with dedicated DSP, 3Msps ADC, and rich analog peripherals for FOC applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The LKS32MC037M6S8 is a high-performance motor control MCU designed for complex FOC and advanced motor control applications.
Featuring a 96MHz 32-bit CPU with dedicated DSP co-processor, this MCU delivers exceptional performance for sensorless FOC algorithms.
The 3Msps dual-sampling ADC and multiple PGA channels enable precise current measurement for high-performance motor control.

faeReview.author: David Wang
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Motor Control Systems
faeReview.content: The LKS32MC037M6S8 is my top recommendation for FOC applications in home appliances. The dedicated DSP with 100ns trigonometric operations makes FOC implementation incredibly efficient - I've measured 20-30% less CPU load compared to generic ARM MCUs running the same algorithm. The dual-sampling ADC is a game-changer for current measurement accuracy, especially at high PWM frequencies. I recently used this MCU in an air conditioner compressor project and the FOC performance was excellent - smooth torque, low noise, and high efficiency. The three PGA channels support both single-shunt and triple-shunt current sensing topologies. One important tip: the DSP is a separate co-processor, so you need to use Linco's DSP library for optimal performance. Don't try to run FOC purely on the ARM core - leverage that DSP!
faeReview.highlight: Dedicated DSP enables efficient FOC with 100ns trigonometric operations

alternativeParts.reason: Linco offers comparable performance at significantly lower cost
Linco provides higher integration with dedicated motor control peripherals

faqs.question: What makes the dedicated DSP in LKS32MC037 special?
faqs.answer: The dedicated DSP in LKS32MC037 provides exceptional motor control performance: (1) Independent co-processor - runs in parallel with ARM core, not sharing execution resources; (2) 100ns trigonometric operations - sin/cos/atan calculations critical for FOC are extremely fast; (3) Hardware divide and square root - 640ns divide, 320ns sqrt; (4) Optimized instruction set - designed specifically for motor control algorithms; (5) Direct memory access - efficient data exchange with CPU. This architecture delivers 3-5x better FOC performance than generic ARM MCUs at the same clock speed. The DSP handles the computationally intensive Park/Clarke transforms while the ARM manages system tasks. Linco provides optimized DSP libraries that abstract the complexity.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Linco DSP library for optimal FOC performance. Don't run FOC purely on ARM core.

faqs.question: How does the dual-sampling ADC improve motor control?
faqs.answer: The dual-sampling ADC in LKS32MC037 provides significant advantages: (1) Simultaneous sampling - two channels sampled at exactly the same time, critical for accurate current measurement in FOC; (2) 3Msps speed - captures fast transient currents during PWM switching; (3) 12-bit resolution - sufficient precision for high-performance control; (4) Synchronized with PWM - ADC triggered at optimal point in PWM cycle for clean measurement; (5) Dual channels - measure two phase currents simultaneously for three-phase systems. This enables precise torque control and smooth motor operation. In FOC applications, accurate current sampling directly impacts efficiency and torque ripple. The dual-sampling capability eliminates the timing skew issues present in sequential ADCs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use dual-sampling for FOC applications. Trigger ADC synchronized with PWM for best results.

faqs.question: What FOC algorithm features are supported?
faqs.answer: LKS32MC037 supports comprehensive FOC algorithm features: (1) Sensorless FOC - sliding mode observer or flux observer for position estimation; (2) Sensored FOC - encoder or resolver interfaces for precise position feedback; (3) MTPA (Maximum Torque Per Ampere) - optimizes current vector for maximum efficiency; (4) Flux Weakening - extends speed range beyond base speed; (5) Field Oriented Control - full vector control with d-q axis decoupling; (6) Space Vector PWM - efficient three-phase voltage generation; (7) Active Damping - vibration suppression algorithms; (8) Parameter Identification - automatic motor parameter measurement. Linco provides reference implementations for all these features. The dedicated DSP and 96MHz CPU provide sufficient performance for 10-20kHz control loop execution.
faqs.decisionGuide: All standard FOC features supported. Contact FAE for advanced algorithm implementation support.

faqs.question: How much Flash and RAM is needed for FOC implementation?
faqs.answer: FOC implementation memory requirements on LKS32MC037: (1) Flash usage - basic FOC library: 15-20KB; full-featured FOC with all options: 25-30KB; application code: 5-10KB; total: 30-40KB typical. The 64KB Flash provides comfortable headroom; (2) RAM usage - FOC data structures: 2-3KB; stack and variables: 1-2KB; ADC buffers: 0.5KB; total: 4-6KB typical. The 8KB RAM is sufficient for most applications; (3) Memory optimization - Linco libraries are optimized for size, unused features can be excluded; (4) Upgrade path - if memory becomes tight, LKS08 series offers 128KB Flash. For most single-motor FOC applications, the LKS32MC037 memory resources are adequate with margin for future enhancements.
faqs.decisionGuide: 64KB Flash / 8KB RAM sufficient for most FOC. Upgrade to LKS08 if more memory needed.

faqs.question: What current sensing topologies are supported?
faqs.answer: LKS32MC037 supports multiple current sensing topologies: (1) Triple-shunt - one resistor per phase, most accurate, requires 3 PGAs; (2) Dual-shunt - resistors on two phases, third calculated, good accuracy with 2 PGAs; (3) Single-shunt - one DC-link resistor, cost-effective, requires advanced sampling timing; (4) MOSFET RDS(on) sensing - uses drain-source resistance for current measurement, no shunt resistors; (5) Isolated current sensors - Hall effect or current transformers for high voltage. The 3-channel PGA supports all topologies. Triple-shunt provides best performance for FOC. Single-shunt is most cost-effective. Linco reference designs demonstrate all topologies with example code.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use triple-shunt for best FOC. Use single-shunt for cost optimization. All supported by LKS37.

products.description: 96MHz motor control MCU with dedicated DSP, 3Msps ADC, and rich analog peripherals for FOC applications.

categories.name: Automotive Grade MCUs
categories.description: AEC-Q100 certified motor control MCUs designed for automotive applications. The LKS32AT series offers high reliability, wide temperature range, and automotive-specific features for EV and automotive motor control.
categories.longDescription: Linco automotive grade MCUs are AEC-Q100 qualified for demanding automotive applications. As an authorized Linco distributor, LiTong provides automotive-grade MCU selection, PPAP documentation support, and application engineering services. The LKS32AT series includes compact AT037 for small motors, enhanced AT039 for medium power, high-performance AT085 for complex control, and premium AT086 with integrated gate drivers. These MCUs feature automotive temperature range (-40°C to +150°C), LIN/CAN interfaces, diagnostic features, and comprehensive safety mechanisms.

series.name: LKS32AT037
series.description: Compact automotive MCU with integrated drivers and LIN

series.name: LKS32AT039
series.description: Enhanced automotive MCU for medium power motors

series.name: LKS32AT085
series.description: High-performance automotive MCU with rich analog

series.name: LKS32AT086
series.description: Premium automotive MCU with 6N gate driver

selectionGuide.title: How to Select Linco Automotive MCUs
selectionGuide.description: Consider motor power, integration requirements, and automotive qualification needs when selecting LKS32AT series.

faqs.question: What AEC-Q100 grade are Linco automotive MCUs?
faqs.answer: Linco LKS32AT series MCUs are qualified to AEC-Q100 Grade 1: (1) Temperature range - -40°C to +150°C ambient operation; (2) Reliability testing - full qualification including HTOL, THB, uHAST, TC, ESD; (3) PPAP documentation - complete Production Part Approval Process documentation available; (4) Traceability - full lot traceability for automotive supply chain; (5) Quality system - manufactured under IATF 16949 certified processes. Grade 1 is the highest automotive grade, suitable for under-hood applications and safety-critical systems. This qualification level meets the requirements of major automotive OEMs and Tier-1 suppliers for motor control applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: AEC-Q100 Grade 1 suitable for all automotive motor control including under-hood applications.

faqs.question: What automotive communication interfaces are supported?
faqs.answer: LKS32AT series supports key automotive communication protocols: (1) LIN (Local Interconnect Network) - LIN 2.x compliant with automatic baud rate detection and address recognition, integrated LIN PHY in AT037/AT039; (2) CAN (Controller Area Network) - CAN 2.0B support for higher bandwidth communication; (3) UART - multiple UARTs for diagnostic and debug; (4) SPI/I2C - for sensor and peripheral communication. The integrated LIN PHY in AT037/AT039 eliminates external transceiver chips, reducing BOM cost. LIN is widely used in automotive body electronics for door modules, seat control, and HVAC. CAN is used for powertrain and chassis systems.
faqs.decisionGuide: AT037/AT039 have integrated LIN PHY. Use AT085/AT086 for CAN applications.

faqs.question: What safety and diagnostic features are included?
faqs.answer: LKS32AT series includes comprehensive automotive safety features: (1) Watchdog Timer - independent watchdog for system monitoring; (2) Clock Monitor - detects clock failure and switches to backup; (3) Voltage Monitor - brown-out detection and overvoltage protection; (4) Temperature Sensor - internal die temperature monitoring; (5) Memory Protection - write protection and ECC on Flash; (6) Fault Input - dedicated hardware fault input for external safety signals; (7) Diagnostic Coverage - extensive self-test capabilities. These features support ASIL requirements for functional safety. The MCUs also include fault injection capabilities for safety system testing during development.
faqs.decisionGuide: Comprehensive safety features support ASIL applications. Contact FAE for functional safety documentation.

faqs.question: What gate driver options are available in automotive MCUs?
faqs.answer: LKS32AT series offers various gate driver integration: (1) AT037/AT039 - 4-phase half-bridge driver with 0.5A source/sink, suitable for small motors up to 2A phase current; (2) AT086 - 6N gate driver with 1.2A source/1.5A sink, supports larger motors up to 20A phase current; (3) AT085 - no integrated driver, use external gate driver for flexibility; (4) Protection features - all drivers include overcurrent protection, shoot-through prevention, and undervoltage lockout. The integrated drivers reduce PCB size and simplify design. For high-power applications, the AT086's 6N driver provides sufficient drive capability for most automotive motors.
faqs.decisionGuide: AT037 for small motors. AT086 with 6N driver for high power. AT085 for external driver flexibility.

faqs.question: How do I get PPAP documentation for automotive projects?
faqs.answer: PPAP (Production Part Approval Process) documentation for LKS32AT series: (1) Available documents - PSW, dimensional results, material certifications, performance test results, process flow, control plan, FMEA; (2) Request process - contact LiTong FAE with your project information and OEM requirements; (3) Lead time - typically 2-4 weeks for complete PPAP package; (4) Custom requirements - special testing or additional documentation can be arranged; (5) Ongoing support - engineering change notifications and quality updates. LiTong works directly with Linco to provide complete automotive qualification support. Early engagement in the design phase ensures all documentation requirements are met for production.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact LiTong FAE early in design phase for PPAP documentation and automotive support.

products.name: Automotive Motor Control MCU
products.shortDescription: AEC-Q100 Grade 1 automotive MCU with 48MHz Cortex-M0, integrated 4-phase driver, LIN PHY, and 5V LDO.
descriptionParagraphs: The LKS32AT037PXL5M6Q9 is an AEC-Q100 Grade 1 certified automotive motor control MCU designed for vehicle applications.
Featuring a 48MHz ARM Cortex-M0 core with integrated 4-phase half-bridge driver and LIN PHY, this MCU provides a highly integrated solution for automotive motors.
The 5V LDO and comprehensive protection features make it ideal for HVAC actuators, active grille shutters, and cooling fans.

faeReview.author: Robert Liu
faeReview.title: Automotive FAE Engineer
faeReview.content: The LKS32AT037PXL5M6Q8 is an excellent choice for small automotive motors. The integration of LIN PHY, gate driver, and LDO in one chip is remarkable - it reduces the BOM from 5-6 chips to just 1 MCU plus MOSFETs. I've used this in several HVAC actuator projects and the LIN auto-addressing feature works flawlessly. The AEC-Q100 Grade 1 qualification gives customers confidence for under-hood applications. One important consideration: the 0.5A gate drive is sufficient for small motors but you'll need external drivers for high-current applications. For active grille shutters and HVAC flaps, this MCU hits the sweet spot of integration and cost. The LIN compliance testing passed without issues in our customer's system.
faeReview.highlight: Highly integrated automotive MCU with LIN PHY and gate driver reduces BOM significantly

alternativeParts.reason: Linco offers comparable integration at lower cost
Linco provides 32-bit performance with higher integration

companionParts.description: Enhanced automotive MCU with more I/O

faqs.question: What automotive applications is the LKS32AT037 best suited for?
faqs.answer: The LKS32AT037 is optimized for small automotive motor applications: (1) HVAC systems - blower motors, blend door actuators, mode door actuators; (2) Thermal management - engine cooling fans, radiator fans, intercooler fans; (3) Body electronics - active grille shutters, charging port covers, mirror adjustment; (4) Fluid pumps - oil pumps, water pumps, vacuum pumps; (5) Valve control - expansion valves, bypass valves. The 0.5A gate drive capability suits motors up to 50W continuous power. The integrated LIN PHY makes it ideal for body electronics connected to LIN bus. For higher power motors, consider AT086 with stronger gate drivers. The compact QFN24 package fits space-constrained automotive modules.
faqs.decisionGuide: Best for small automotive motors <50W with LIN communication. Use AT086 for higher power.

faqs.question: How does the LIN auto-addressing feature work?
faqs.answer: The LKS32AT037 LIN auto-addressing feature: (1) Automatic detection - MCU automatically detects LIN baud rate (1k-20kbps) during communication; (2) Address assignment - supports automatic slave address assignment per LIN 2.x specification; (3) Sleep/wake - integrated LIN sleep mode with remote wake-up capability; (4) Frame handling - hardware LIN frame processing reduces CPU load; (5) Error detection - automatic LIN checksum and error detection. This eliminates manual configuration and enables plug-and-play installation in automotive systems. Multiple identical actuators can share the same hardware design with different LIN addresses assigned during assembly. The integrated PHY meets automotive EMC requirements without external components.
faqs.decisionGuide: Auto-addressing simplifies production. Multiple actuators can use same hardware with different addresses.

faqs.question: What motor types can be driven with the integrated driver?
faqs.answer: The integrated 4-phase half-bridge driver supports: (1) Brushed DC motors - H-bridge configuration with PWM speed control; (2) Stepper motors - bipolar stepper control with microstepping; (3) Single-phase BLDC - for simple fan motors; (4) Three-phase BLDC - with external MOSFETs for higher current. The 0.5A source/sink current is suitable for small motors up to 20W. The driver includes shoot-through protection, undervoltage lockout, and overcurrent protection. For three-phase BLDC, the driver controls low-side MOSFETs while high-side uses external drivers or bootstrap circuits. The integrated driver eliminates external pre-driver chips, saving PCB space and cost.
faqs.decisionGuide: Supports brushed DC, stepper, and small BLDC motors. Use external drivers for high-current three-phase motors.

faqs.question: What is the qualification testing for AEC-Q100 Grade 1?
faqs.answer: AEC-Q100 Grade 1 qualification includes extensive testing: (1) HTOL (High Temperature Operating Life) - 1000 hours at 150°C; (2) THB (Temperature Humidity Bias) - 1000 hours at 85°C/85%RH; (3) uHAST (Unbiased Highly Accelerated Stress Test) - 96 hours at 130°C/85%RH; (4) TCT (Temperature Cycling Test) - 1000 cycles -65°C to +150°C; (5) ESD - 2kV HBM, 750V CDM, 15kV air discharge; (6) Latch-up - 100mA current injection; (7) Early Life Failure Rate - 48 hours burn-in. This testing ensures reliability in harsh automotive environments. The qualification is performed on 3 production lots with statistical analysis. PPAP documentation includes all test results and process information required by automotive OEMs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Full AEC-Q100 Grade 1 testing ensures automotive reliability. PPAP available through LiTong.

faqs.question: How do I implement functional safety with LKS32AT037?
faqs.answer: Functional safety implementation with LKS32AT037: (1) Hardware features - watchdog timer, clock monitor, voltage monitor, temperature sensor, memory protection; (2) Diagnostic coverage - implement periodic self-tests for CPU, memory, and peripherals; (3) Safe states - define safe state on fault detection (motor coast, brake, or controlled shutdown); (4) Redundancy - use dual-channel current sensing or position feedback for higher ASIL; (5) Software - follow MISRA-C guidelines, implement defensive programming; (6) Documentation - FMEA, safety manual, and safety case development. The MCU supports up to ASIL-B with appropriate system design. For higher ASIL requirements, external safety monitoring may be needed. Linco provides safety application notes and can support safety documentation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Supports ASIL-B with proper system design. Contact FAE for functional safety support and documentation.

products.description: AEC-Q100 Grade 1 automotive MCU with 48MHz Cortex-M0, integrated 4-phase driver, LIN PHY, and 5V LDO.

products.name: High-Performance Automotive MCU
products.shortDescription: AEC-Q100 Grade 1 automotive MCU with 96MHz CPU, 6N gate driver, and 1.2A/1.5A drive capability for high-power motors.
descriptionParagraphs: The LKS32AT086N8Q9 is a premium automotive motor control MCU with integrated 6N gate driver for high-power motor applications.
Featuring a 96MHz CPU with dedicated DSP and 1.2A source/1.5A sink gate drive capability, this MCU can drive large automotive motors directly.
The comprehensive analog peripherals and automotive-grade reliability make it ideal for engine cooling fans, oil pumps, and HVAC compressors.

faeReview.author: James Zhang
faeReview.title: Senior Automotive FAE
faeReview.content: The LKS32AT086N8Q9 is the flagship automotive MCU in Linco's portfolio. The 1.2A/1.5A gate drive is powerful enough to drive even large MOSFETs for high-current motors - I've successfully used it for 200W+ engine cooling fans without external drivers. The 6N driver with integrated protection features (shoot-through prevention, UVLO, overcurrent) saves significant design effort. The 96MHz CPU with DSP handles FOC for these high-power motors with ease. One critical advantage over competitors: the high-voltage floating drive supports up to 200V, making it suitable for 48V mild-hybrid systems. For automotive HVAC compressors and large cooling fans, this MCU provides the best integration and performance. The AEC-Q100 Grade 1 qualification and comprehensive PPAP support make it production-ready for automotive OEMs.
faeReview.highlight: Powerful 6N gate driver with 1.2A/1.5A capability for high-power automotive motors

alternativeParts.reason: Linco offers higher drive current and voltage with MCU integration

comparison.Features: External MCU needed = Single chip (Linco advantage)

alternativeParts.reason: Linco provides automotive qualification with integrated MCU

faqs.question: What motor power can the AT086 drive with integrated 6N driver?
faqs.answer: The LKS32AT086 6N gate driver capability: (1) Gate drive current - 1.2A source, 1.5A sink per channel, sufficient for large MOSFETs; (2) Motor power - can drive motors up to 500W continuous with appropriate MOSFETs; (3) Voltage range - supports 12V, 24V, and 48V systems with 200V floating drive capability; (4) MOSFET gate charge - can drive MOSFETs with up to 200nC gate charge at 20kHz PWM; (5) Applications tested - 200W+ engine cooling fans, 300W HVAC compressors, 150W oil pumps. The strong drive capability ensures fast MOSFET switching for low switching losses. For very high power (>500W), external gate drivers may still be needed, but the AT086 covers most automotive motor applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Suitable for motors up to 500W. Covers most automotive motor applications without external drivers.

faqs.question: What protection features does the 6N gate driver include?
faqs.answer: The integrated 6N gate driver includes comprehensive protection: (1) Shoot-through prevention - hardware interlock prevents simultaneous high/low side conduction; (2) Undervoltage Lockout (UVLO) - disables driver if supply voltage too low to prevent MOSFET damage; (3) Overcurrent protection - fast overcurrent detection with programmable threshold; (4) Overtemperature protection - driver shutdown on over temperature; (5) Programmable dead-time - configurable delay between high/low side switching; (6) Fault reporting - dedicated fault output pin for system monitoring. These protections are critical for automotive reliability. The shoot-through prevention alone can save MOSFETs during transient conditions. All protection features are independent of CPU operation for fast response.
faqs.decisionGuide: Comprehensive protection enables reliable automotive operation. Configure thresholds based on your MOSFETs.

faqs.question: Can AT086 support 48V mild-hybrid systems?
faqs.answer: Yes, LKS32AT086 supports 48V mild-hybrid applications: (1) Voltage rating - 200V floating drive capability supports 48V systems with margin; (2) MOSFET compatibility - gate drive suitable for 60V-100V MOSFETs used in 48V systems; (3) Power capability - can drive high-power 48V motors for belt-driven starter generators, cooling systems; (4) Isolation - bootstrap operation provides inherent high-side isolation; (5) Protection - overvoltage protection handles 48V system transients. 48V systems are becoming common for mild hybrids to power high-power accessories. The AT086's high voltage capability and strong gate drive make it well-suited for these applications. Reference designs are available for 48V motor control.
faqs.decisionGuide: 200V rating supports 48V mild-hybrid applications. Contact FAE for 48V reference designs.

faqs.question: How does the DSP accelerate FOC algorithms?
faqs.answer: The dedicated DSP in AT086 provides significant FOC acceleration: (1) Trigonometric functions - sin/cos/atan in 100ns vs 5-10us on ARM core; (2) Park/Clarke transforms - hardware-accelerated coordinate transformations; (3) PI controllers - optimized execution for current and speed loops; (4) Vector calculations - hardware support for 3-phase vector math; (5) Parallel operation - DSP runs independently while ARM handles system tasks. This enables 10-20kHz FOC control loops with low CPU load. For high-power automotive motors where smooth torque and low noise are critical, the DSP performance ensures precise control. The Linco FOC library leverages the DSP for optimal performance - don't run FOC purely on the ARM core.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Linco DSP library for optimal FOC. DSP provides 50-100x acceleration for trigonometric functions.

faqs.question: What thermal management is needed for high-power applications?
faqs.answer: Thermal management for AT086 high-power applications: (1) Package thermal resistance - Theta-JA approximately 35°C/W for QFN52, varies with PCB design; (2) Power dissipation - MCU typically dissipates 0.5-1W at 96MHz, gate driver adds 0.1-0.3W depending on switching frequency; (3) PCB design - use thermal vias under package, copper pours for heat spreading; (4) Ambient temperature - ensure case temperature stays below 150°C at maximum ambient; (5) MOSFET placement - keep high-power MOSFETs thermally isolated from MCU or provide adequate cooling. For continuous high-power operation, thermal modeling is recommended. The automotive temperature range (-40°C to +150°C) provides margin for harsh environments. LiTong FAE can provide thermal design guidance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use thermal vias and copper pours. Keep Tcase < 150°C. Contact FAE for thermal modeling support.

products.description: AEC-Q100 Grade 1 automotive MCU with 96MHz CPU, 6N gate driver, and 1.2A/1.5A drive capability for high-power motors.

seoKeywords: motor control automotive grade mcu distributor

categories.name: Gate Drivers
categories.description: High and low voltage gate drivers for MOSFET and IGBT power stages. Linco gate drivers provide reliable drive capability with integrated protection features for motor control applications.
categories.longDescription: Linco gate drivers are designed for motor control power stage applications. As an authorized Linco distributor, LiTong provides gate driver selection guidance and application support. The product range includes low-voltage half-bridge drivers for 12V-24V systems and high-voltage drivers for 48V-400V applications. These drivers feature integrated bootstrap diodes, shoot-through protection, and undervoltage lockout for reliable operation.

selectionGuide.title: How to Select Linco Gate Drivers
selectionGuide.description: Consider voltage range, drive current, and protection features when selecting Linco gate drivers.

faqs.question: What drive current do I need for my MOSFETs?
faqs.answer: Gate drive current selection depends on MOSFET characteristics: (1) Gate charge (Qg) - total gate charge from datasheet, typically 10-200nC; (2) Switching time - desired rise/fall time, typically 50-200ns; (3) Calculation - Igate = Qg / trise; example: 100nC / 100ns = 1A drive current; (4) Margin - select driver with 20-50% more current than calculated; (5) Frequency - higher PWM frequencies need faster switching. For small MOSFETs (Qg < 50nC), 0.5A drivers suffice. For large MOSFETs (Qg > 100nC), 1A+ drivers are needed. Linco offers 0.5A, 1A, and 2A drive options to match different MOSFET requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate required current from Qg and desired switching time. Add 20-50% margin.

faqs.question: What is shoot-through protection and why is it important?
faqs.answer: Shoot-through protection prevents destructive current flow: (1) Problem - in half-bridge, both high and low side MOSFETs conducting simultaneously creates short circuit; (2) Cause - timing overlap during switching, insufficient dead-time, or noise; (3) Solution - hardware shoot-through protection prevents simultaneous conduction; (4) Implementation - driver monitors both outputs and forces dead-time; (5) Importance - shoot-through can destroy MOSFETs in microseconds, causes EMI, reduces efficiency. Linco drivers include hardware shoot-through protection independent of external control. This is essential for reliable motor drive operation. The protection adds inherent dead-time between high and low side switching.
faqs.decisionGuide: Always use drivers with shoot-through protection. Essential for reliable half-bridge operation.

faqs.question: What is bootstrap operation in high-side drivers?
faqs.answer: Bootstrap operation powers high-side gate drive: (1) Challenge - high-side MOSFET source floats with motor voltage, needs gate drive above source; (2) Solution - bootstrap capacitor charged from low-side supply when low-side is on; (3) Operation - capacitor provides floating supply for high-side driver; (4) Requirements - sufficient capacitor value, refresh time, voltage rating; (5) Limitations - duty cycle limited (typically <95%), initial charging needed. Bootstrap is cost-effective for motor drives where 100% duty cycle isn't required. For continuous high-side conduction, isolated supplies are needed. Linco drivers include integrated bootstrap diodes for simplified design.
faqs.decisionGuide: Bootstrap suitable for most motor drives. Use isolated supply if >95% duty cycle needed.

faqs.question: What UVLO protection does Linco drivers provide?
faqs.answer: Undervoltage Lockout (UVLO) protection in Linco drivers: (1) Purpose - prevents MOSFET operation with insufficient gate voltage; (2) Thresholds - typically 8-10V for turn-on, 7-9V for turn-off with hysteresis; (3) Behavior - driver outputs held low when VCC below threshold; (4) Importance - insufficient gate voltage causes high RDS(on), excessive heating, MOSFET damage; (5) Hysteresis - prevents oscillation near threshold. UVLO is essential for reliable operation during power-up, brown-out conditions, and transient events. Linco drivers have both VCC UVLO and bootstrap UVLO (for high-side). UVLO ensures MOSFETs are fully enhanced before operation begins.
faqs.decisionGuide: UVLO essential for reliable operation. Ensure supply voltage stays above UVLO threshold.

faqs.question: How do I select between integrated and standalone drivers?
faqs.answer: Integrated vs standalone driver selection: (1) Integrated drivers (in MCU) - advantages: reduced BOM, smaller PCB, simpler design; disadvantages: limited drive current, MCU heat, less flexibility; (2) Standalone drivers - advantages: higher drive current, better thermal management, more protection features, design flexibility; disadvantages: additional component cost, PCB space; (3) Decision factors - motor power (<50W integrated OK, >100W standalone), MOSFET size (large Qg needs standalone), thermal constraints, cost sensitivity. Linco offers both options - LKS08 has integrated drivers for small motors, standalone drivers available for high-power applications. Many designs use integrated drivers for prototyping, standalone for production high-power.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use integrated for small motors <50W. Use standalone for high power or large MOSFETs.

products.name: 6N Gate Driver IC
products.shortDescription: Three-phase 6N gate driver with 1.5A drive current, integrated bootstrap diodes, and comprehensive protection.
descriptionParagraphs: The LKS_GD_6N_1A5 is a three-phase 6N gate driver designed for motor control power stages.
Featuring 1.5A source/sink drive capability and integrated bootstrap diodes, this driver simplifies three-phase inverter design.
The comprehensive protection features including shoot-through prevention and UVLO ensure reliable operation.

faeReview.author: Kevin Wu
faeReview.title: Power Electronics FAE
faeReview.content: The LKS_GD_6N_1A5 is a solid choice for three-phase motor drives. The 1.5A drive current handles most MOSFETs up to 100A without issues. I particularly like the integrated bootstrap diodes - saves 6 external diodes and reduces PCB space significantly. The shoot-through protection has saved my customers from MOSFET failures multiple times during development. One design tip: size the bootstrap capacitors properly - typically 10-47uF depending on switching frequency and high-side MOSFET gate charge. The UVLO thresholds are well-chosen at around 10V, ensuring MOSFETs are fully enhanced. For general-purpose motor drives and inverters, this driver offers good performance at competitive pricing.
faeReview.highlight: Integrated bootstrap diodes and 1.5A drive simplify three-phase inverter design

alternativeParts.reason: Linco offers much higher drive current with integrated bootstrap
alternativeParts.useCase: High-power motor drives requiring strong gate drive

alternativeParts.reason: Linco offers higher voltage rating at better price

faqs.question: What bootstrap capacitor value should I use?
faqs.answer: Bootstrap capacitor sizing calculation: (1) Minimum capacitance - Cboot > 2 × (2Qg + Iq/fsw + Qls), where Qg is high-side MOSFET gate charge, Iq is quiescent current, fsw is switching frequency, Qls is level shift charge; (2) Typical values - 10uF for small MOSFETs (Qg<50nC), 22uF for medium (Qg 50-100nC), 47uF for large (Qg>100nC); (3) Voltage rating - 2× bootstrap voltage, typically 25V or 35V; (4) Type - X5R or X7R ceramic capacitor for low ESR; (5) Placement - close to driver pins with short traces. Undersized capacitor causes insufficient gate voltage and high switching losses. Oversized capacitor slows charging and may exceed charging time limits.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with 22uF for typical applications. Calculate based on Qg and switching frequency.

faqs.question: How do I prevent gate oscillations and ringing?
faqs.answer: Gate oscillation prevention techniques: (1) Gate resistor - series resistor (typically 5-20Ω) dampens oscillations, limits di/dt; (2) Loop inductance - minimize gate drive loop area, use Kelvin connections; (3) PCB layout - keep gate traces short and wide, avoid vias in gate path; (4) Gate capacitance - small capacitor (100pF-1nF) from gate to source can dampen high-frequency ringing; (5) Ferrite bead - series ferrite bead blocks high-frequency oscillations. Gate oscillations cause EMI, excessive switching losses, and MOSFET damage. They are caused by parasitic inductance resonating with MOSFET capacitance. Proper layout is more important than component values - minimize loop inductance first.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 10Ω gate resistor. Minimize loop inductance through proper PCB layout.

faqs.question: What dead-time should I set for my application?
faqs.answer: Dead-time selection guidelines: (1) Purpose - prevents shoot-through by ensuring one MOSFET is fully off before other turns on; (2) Minimum - must exceed MOSFET turn-off time (typically 100-500ns); (3) Maximum - excessive dead-time causes body diode conduction, efficiency loss; (4) Typical values - 500ns to 2us for IGBT, 200ns to 1us for MOSFET; (5) Temperature - MOSFETs switch slower at high temperature, increase dead-time margin; (6) Optimization - start conservative (1-2us), reduce while monitoring efficiency and temperature. Linco drivers have programmable dead-time. The optimal dead-time balances shoot-through protection with efficiency. Measure switching waveforms to verify adequate dead-time.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with 1us dead-time. Reduce while monitoring efficiency. Never below 200ns.

faqs.question: How do I interface the driver with my MCU?
faqs.answer: MCU interface to gate driver: (1) Input signals - typically 3.3V or 5V logic level inputs for HIN/LIN (high/low side inputs); (2) Level translation - Linco drivers accept 3.3V logic even with 12V supply; (3) PWM frequency - driver supports up to 100kHz PWM, limited by propagation delay; (4) Isolation - for high-voltage applications, use optocouplers or digital isolators between MCU and driver; (5) Fault feedback - connect driver fault output to MCU GPIO for protection monitoring; (6) Enable control - use driver enable pin for system-level shutdown. The interface is straightforward for low-voltage systems. For high-voltage or safety-critical applications, add isolation between MCU and driver.
faqs.decisionGuide: Direct connection for low voltage. Use isolation for high voltage or safety applications.

faqs.question: What thermal considerations apply to gate drivers?
faqs.answer: Gate driver thermal management: (1) Power dissipation - Pd = Qg × Vgs × fsw × 6 (for 6N driver), example: 100nC × 12V × 20kHz × 6 = 144mW; (2) Junction temperature - Tj = Ta + Pd × Theta-JA, ensure Tj < 125°C; (3) PCB design - thermal vias under package, copper area for heat spreading; (4) Switching frequency - higher fsw increases dissipation significantly; (5) MOSFET gate charge - larger Qg increases driver dissipation. Gate drivers typically don't require heatsinks, but proper PCB thermal design is important. The main heat source is charging/discharging MOSFET gate capacitance. For very high frequencies (>50kHz) or large MOSFETs, verify thermal calculations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate power dissipation from Qg and fsw. Ensure Tj < 125°C with proper PCB design.

categories.name: Power Management ICs
categories.description: Integrated power management solutions including LDOs, DC-DC converters, and voltage regulators for motor control systems. Linco PMICs provide stable power supply with high efficiency and comprehensive protection.
categories.longDescription: Linco power management ICs are designed for motor control system power requirements. As an authorized Linco distributor, LiTong provides PMIC selection guidance and power design support. The product range includes 5V LDOs for MCU supply, buck converters for efficient voltage conversion, and multi-output PMICs for complete system power solutions.

series.name: LDO Series
series.description: Low dropout regulators for MCU and analog supply

series.name: DC-DC Series
series.description: Buck converters for efficient power conversion

selectionGuide.title: How to Select Linco Power Management ICs
selectionGuide.description: Consider voltage requirements, current needs, efficiency, and thermal constraints when selecting PMICs.

faqs.question: When should I use LDO vs DC-DC converter?
faqs.answer: LDO vs DC-DC selection criteria: (1) Voltage difference - small dropout (<2V) use LDO, large difference use DC-DC; (2) Efficiency - LDO efficiency = Vout/Vin, DC-DC typically 85-95%; (3) Noise sensitivity - LDO has lower noise, better for analog/RF; (4) Current - high current (>500mA) favors DC-DC efficiency; (5) Cost - LDO simpler and cheaper; (6) EMI - LDO generates no EMI, DC-DC requires filtering. For motor control: use LDO for MCU supply (low current, needs clean power), use DC-DC for power stage (high current, efficiency critical). Many designs use both - DC-DC for main conversion, LDO post-regulation for sensitive circuits.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use LDO for low current, low noise, small dropout. Use DC-DC for high current, large voltage difference.

faqs.question: What is dropout voltage in LDOs?
faqs.answer: LDO dropout voltage explained: (1) Definition - minimum voltage difference between input and output for regulation, typically 200mV-1V; (2) Importance - determines minimum input voltage for desired output; example: 3.3V output with 500mV dropout requires 3.8V minimum input; (3) Factors - dropout depends on load current and LDO design, PMOS LDOs have lower dropout than NMOS; (4) Headroom - always maintain margin above dropout for line transient response; (5) Battery applications - low dropout critical for maximizing battery life. Linco LDOs feature low dropout (200-500mV) for efficient operation. Check dropout vs load current curves in datasheet.
faqs.decisionGuide: Ensure Vin > Vout + dropout + margin. Select low dropout for battery or tight voltage applications.

faqs.question: How do I calculate power dissipation in LDOs?
faqs.answer: LDO power dissipation calculation: (1) Formula - Pd = (Vin - Vout) × Iout; (2) Example - 12V to 5V at 100mA: (12-5) × 0.1 = 0.7W; (3) Thermal - Tj = Ta + Pd × Theta-JA, must keep Tj < maximum rating; (4) Efficiency - Pout/Pin = Vout/Vin, 5V/12V = 42% in example; (5) Heat sinking - may need copper area or heatsink for high dissipation. LDOs are inefficient when Vin >> Vout. For high current or large voltage differences, use DC-DC converter instead. Calculate worst-case dissipation at maximum Vin and Iout. If Tj exceeds rating, reduce load, lower Vin, add heat sinking, or switch to DC-DC.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate Pd = (Vin-Vout)×Iout. Ensure Tj < Tmax. Use DC-DC if dissipation too high.

faqs.question: What protection features do Linco PMICs include?
faqs.answer: Linco PMIC protection features: (1) Overcurrent Protection (OCP) - limits output current during short circuit or overload; (2) Overvoltage Protection (OVP) - prevents output voltage exceeding safe levels; (3) Undervoltage Lockout (UVLO) - disables output when input too low; (4) Thermal Shutdown - turns off at overtemperature, auto-restarts when cooled; (5) Soft-start - gradual output ramp prevents inrush current; (6) Reverse Current Protection - prevents damage from reverse polarity. These protections ensure reliable operation and prevent damage during fault conditions. Automotive-grade PMICs have enhanced protection and diagnostics.
faqs.decisionGuide: All Linco PMICs include comprehensive protection. Select automotive grade for enhanced reliability.

faqs.question: How do I select the right output capacitor?
faqs.answer: Output capacitor selection guidelines: (1) Capacitance - larger value improves transient response and stability, typical 10-100uF; (2) ESR - low ESR ceramic preferred for LDOs, critical for stability; (3) Type - X5R/X7R ceramic for low ESR, tantalum or electrolytic for bulk capacitance; (4) Voltage rating - 2× output voltage minimum; (5) Placement - close to output pins with short traces; (6) Temperature - X7R better than X5R for high temperature. Output capacitor affects stability, transient response, and noise. Insufficient capacitance causes oscillation and poor load transient response. Follow datasheet recommendations for minimum capacitance and ESR.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 22uF X7R ceramic minimum. Place close to output pins. Check datasheet for specific requirements.

products.name: 5V LDO Regulator
products.shortDescription: 500mA 5V LDO with low dropout, thermal protection, and enable control for MCU power supply.
descriptionParagraphs: The LKS_PMIC_5V_500 is a 500mA 5V LDO regulator designed for MCU and digital circuit power supply.
Featuring low dropout voltage of 350mV at 500mA and comprehensive thermal protection, this LDO provides reliable operation.
The enable pin allows power sequencing and system-level power management.

faeReview.author: Lisa Huang
faeReview.title: Power Management FAE
faeReview.content: The LKS_PMIC_5V_500 is a solid general-purpose LDO for motor control systems. The 350mV dropout at 500mA is competitive - you can operate from 6V input with 5V output even at full load. I've used this extensively for MCU supplies in 12V and 24V motor drive systems. The 70dB PSRR at 1kHz provides clean power for analog circuits and ADC references. One important consideration: power dissipation at high input voltage - at 24V input, 5V output, 100mA load, you're dissipating 1.9W. Make sure your PCB thermal design can handle it. The SOT-223 package needs copper area for heatsinking. For very high input voltages or currents, consider using a buck converter instead. Overall, reliable and cost-effective for typical applications.
faeReview.highlight: Low dropout and high PSRR make it ideal for MCU supply in motor control systems

alternativeParts.reason: Linco offers much lower dropout and better PSRR
Linco offers competitive performance at lower cost
alternativeParts.useCase: Cost-sensitive applications requiring good LDO performance

faqs.question: What input capacitor is recommended for this LDO?
faqs.answer: Input capacitor recommendations for LKS_PMIC_5V_500: (1) Minimum capacitance - 10uF ceramic for stability; (2) Type - X5R or X7R ceramic capacitor, 25V or higher rating; (3) Purpose - supplies transient current during load steps, filters input noise; (4) Placement - close to input pin with short traces; (5) Additional - add 100nF ceramic in parallel for high-frequency decoupling; (6) ESR - ceramic capacitors have very low ESR, ideal for LDOs. If input source is distant or has high impedance, increase capacitance to 22-47uF. Input capacitor prevents oscillation and improves transient response. Always follow datasheet minimum requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 10uF X5R/X7R ceramic minimum. Add 100nF for high frequency. Place close to input pin.

faqs.question: How does the enable pin work and how should I use it?
faqs.answer: Enable pin operation and usage: (1) Function - turns LDO on/off, active high typically; (2) Threshold - enable voltage > 1.2V turns on, < 0.4V turns off; (3) Current - minimal current required, can be driven directly from MCU GPIO; (4) Sequencing - use for power-up sequencing in multi-rail systems; (5) Shutdown current - typically < 1uA when disabled, important for battery applications; (6) Soft-start - internal soft-start when enabled prevents inrush current. Usage examples: (1) MCU control - GPIO controls LDO for power management; (2) Power sequencing - enable 5V before 3.3V; (3) System shutdown - disable non-critical rails in standby. Leave enable floating or tie to input if not used.
faqs.decisionGuide: Connect to MCU GPIO for power management. Use for power sequencing in multi-rail systems.

faqs.question: What is PSRR and why does it matter?
faqs.answer: PSRR (Power Supply Rejection Ratio) explained: (1) Definition - measure of how well LDO rejects input voltage variations, expressed in dB; (2) Higher is better - 70dB means input ripple is attenuated by 3000x; (3) Frequency dependent - typically high at low frequencies, decreases at high frequencies; (4) Importance - prevents input noise from affecting output, critical for analog circuits and ADC references; (5) Motor control - switching noise from motor drive can couple to supply, high PSRR maintains clean MCU power. The LKS_PMIC_5V_500's 70dB PSRR at 1kHz provides excellent noise rejection for motor control applications. Check PSRR vs frequency curve for your application's noise spectrum.
faqs.decisionGuide: High PSRR important for analog circuits and ADC references. 70dB provides excellent rejection.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the maximum allowable ambient temperature?
faqs.answer: Maximum ambient temperature calculation: (1) Formula - Tambient_max = Tj_max - Pd × Theta-JA; (2) Example - Tj_max = 125°C, Pd = 0.7W, Theta-JA = 50°C/W: 125 - 0.7 × 50 = 90°C; (3) Theta-JA - depends on package and PCB design, SOT-223 typically 40-60°C/W with minimal copper, 20-30°C/W with good heatsinking; (4) Power dissipation - Pd = (Vin - Vout) × Iout, use worst-case values; (5) Margin - keep 10-20°C margin below calculated maximum. If calculated Tambient is too low for your application: increase copper area, add heatsink, reduce load current, reduce input voltage, or use DC-DC converter instead.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate Tambient_max = Tj_max - Pd×Theta-JA. Add copper area if temperature too low.

faqs.question: Can I use this LDO for battery-powered applications?
faqs.answer: Battery application considerations: (1) Dropout voltage - 350mV at 500mA means battery can discharge to 5.35V before losing regulation; (2) Quiescent current - 2mA is moderate, will drain battery over time; (3) Shutdown current - < 1uA when disabled, good for standby; (4) Efficiency - poor for large Vin-Vout differences, e.g., 12V to 5V is only 42%; (5) Use cases - good for short battery life or when efficiency not critical; not ideal for long-life battery applications. For battery applications: use if battery voltage close to output (e.g., 6V battery to 5V), use shutdown mode when not active, consider switching to buck converter for better efficiency. The low dropout helps maximize battery life by allowing deeper discharge.
faqs.decisionGuide: OK for battery if Vin close to Vout. Use shutdown mode. Consider buck for efficiency-critical apps.
